notes: Tracks planned and in-progress features for the Hearth agent harness. Mark passes=true only after manual end-to-end verification. Do not remove or rename entries — add new ones at the bottom of the relevant category. Priorities: 1=highest, 3=lowest. New entries include an optional 'phase' field: 'v1' = must ship before public launch, 'v1.1' = first post-launch sprint, 'later' = longer-term roadmap. V1 must-ship features are listed first.

features.title: Cloud deployment — Vercel (web) + Railway (API) + Neon (Postgres)
features.description: Deploy the production stack so Hearth is accessible from a public URL. Recommended split: Next.js web on Vercel (zero-config for Next.js), FastAPI API on Railway (long-running process, no serverless adaptation needed), Neon managed Postgres (Vercel's preferred Postgres partner, generous free tier). Railway config: start command is `uvicorn life_dashboard.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT`, root dir is api/, build command installs from requirements.txt. All env vars (SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_URL, FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY, TELLER_*, RESEND_API_KEY, ALLOWED_ORIGINS, etc.) go in Railway. Vercel config: NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL points at the Railway public URL. CORS on the API must include the Vercel domain. HTTPS is automatic on both platforms. Run `alembic upgrade head` against Neon before first boot.
steps: Provision Neon Postgres — copy DATABASE_URL and verify `alembic upgrade head` runs cleanly against it
Create Railway project pointing at api/ — set start command, add all env vars, deploy
Verify GET /health (or GET /docs) returns 200 from the Railway public URL
Deploy Next.js to Vercel — set NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL to the Railway URL, deploy
Verify CORS: open the Vercel URL in a browser and complete signup — no CORS errors in the console
Verify full auth round-trip: log in, load the dashboard, confirm API calls succeed with no network errors
Verify field-level encryption works: save an AI API key, confirm it persists and AI chat responds
Configure a custom domain on Vercel if desired

features.title: Transactional email — Resend integration + forgot password flow
features.description: Wire up transactional email using Resend (resend.com — generous free tier, excellent developer experience, simple Python SDK). Required for: password reset, household invitations (household-001), and future digest emails. Backend: add resend to requirements.txt; add RESEND_API_KEY and EMAIL_FROM env vars to settings.py; create core/email.py with a send_email(to, subject, html) helper that calls resend.Emails.send(); create an email template for password reset. Auth domain: add a POST /auth/forgot-password endpoint that generates a signed time-limited reset token (HMAC of user_id + expiry stored in a password_reset_tokens table or as a signed JWT), sends a reset email, and returns 204 (no indication of whether the email exists). Add POST /auth/reset-password that validates the token and updates the password hash. Frontend: add 'Forgot password?' link on the login page, a /forgot-password page with email input, and a /reset-password?token=... page with new-password form.
steps: Add RESEND_API_KEY and EMAIL_FROM to api/.env and settings.py
pip install resend — verify import in a Python shell
POST /auth/forgot-password with a real email — verify no 500, verify the email arrives
Click the reset link in the email — verify the /reset-password page loads with the token pre-filled
Submit a new password — verify login with the new password succeeds
Submit POST /auth/forgot-password with a non-existent email — verify 204 (no user enumeration)
Use the reset link a second time — verify it is rejected (token is single-use or expired)

features.title: APScheduler background jobs — Teller sync, profile refresh, data retention
features.description: Set up APScheduler (already a dependency from coach-007) as a shared background scheduler wired into the FastAPI lifespan. Three initial jobs: (1) Teller daily sync — POST /budget/teller/sync-all equivalent run as a background job daily at 04:00 local; iterates all households with linked Teller accounts; (2) Weekly profile refresh — already designed in coach-007 as run_scheduled_profile_refresh_all(), fires Sunday at 03:00; (3) Monthly data retention purge — runs the purge_old_transactions() function from budget-021 on the first day of each month. Each job is wrapped in its own try/except and logs aggregate results. Job runner logs to a structured log line per job completion. For cloud deployment (Railway), the scheduler runs in the same process as the API — no separate worker needed at this scale. Note: the coach digest scheduler already exists for per-user digest delivery; this feature adds the household-level background infrastructure jobs.
steps: Restart the API — verify the boot log shows all three jobs registered with their cron schedules
Manually invoke the Teller sync job function from a Python shell — verify it runs and logs results
Manually invoke run_scheduled_profile_refresh_all() — verify it logs {users, applied, skip, error} counts
Verify no job failure blocks API boot or request handling — kill a job mid-run and confirm the API continues serving
Verify each job's try/except catches per-item errors without aborting the whole run

features.description: Make Hearth installable as a Progressive Web App on iOS and Android and ensure the UI is usable on a 375px viewport. Tasks: (1) Complete web/public/manifest.json with correct name, short_name, icons (192×192 and 512×512 PNG), theme_color, background_color, display: standalone, start_url; (2) Verify Next.js is serving the manifest and meta tags correctly (apple-touch-icon, mobile-web-app-capable, etc.); (3) Add a basic service worker (via next-pwa or a custom one) that caches the app shell so the home screen app loads instantly and shows a useful offline screen rather than a blank page; (4) Responsive layout audit — open every major page at 375px width in Chrome DevTools and fix overflow, truncation, and touch target issues; (5) Test 'Add to Home Screen' on iOS Safari and Android Chrome — verify the installed app launches in standalone mode with the correct icon and no address bar.

Run Lighthouse PWA audit — verify installability criteria all pass

Verify touch targets are at least 44×44px on mobile (buttons, checkboxes, row taps)

Beyond empty states, first-visit hints appear as dismissible callout banners on key pages the first time a user navigates there — one sentence, zero jargon, a link to docs or a video if available. Examples: Budget page first visit → 'Start by importing a bank statement or connecting your bank account.' Habits page first visit → 'Habits track recurring behaviours. Daily streaks and weekly completion rates appear here as you check in.' Hints are tracked per-member in a dismissed_hints JSONB column on member settings (or localStorage for stateless hints). features.title: Habit cadence UX cleanup + optional scheduled_time
features.description: The current habit cadence form has two problems: (1) the 'days of week' multi-selector appears for daily habits (meaningless — daily means every day), and (2) weekly habits show both a 'days of week' selector AND a 'times per week' number input (redundant — if you pick Monday and Wednesday, that is twice per week). Fix the form logic: Daily — no day selector, no times input; the cadence is simply 'every day'. Weekly — show only a 'Which days?' multi-select (M T W T F S S chips); remove the times-per-week number input entirely (completion frequency is derived from the selected days). Monthly — show a 'Which days of the month?' input (day numbers, e.g. 1st and 15th) OR a 'Nth weekday' picker (e.g. first Monday). Also add an optional scheduled_time (HH:MM, 12h display) field to the habit form for all cadences. scheduled_time is stored in the cadence JSONB blob. The calendar view (calendar-002) should use scheduled_time to render habit chips in the correct time slot rather than as all-day items when a time is set.
steps: Open the new habit sheet and select 'Daily' — verify no days-of-week selector appears
Select 'Weekly' — verify only a days-of-week chip selector appears (no times-per-week number input)
Select Monday and Wednesday for a weekly habit — verify it saves and the completion tracker shows Mon/Wed slots

features.title: Category groups — collapsible category sections
features.description:
Allow budget categories to be organized into named groups. Groups are collapsible in the UI. Each category belongs to one group; ungrouped categories fall into a default 'Other' bucket. Requires a new budget_category_groups table with a sort_order and name, and a group_id FK on budget_categories. The /budget/categories response should nest categories inside their group. On first setup, seed the default groups and assignments listed below.

Proposed default groups (inspired by YNAB, adapted for Hearth):

Group 1 — Fixed Monthly (sort 1): Immediate recurring obligations, roughly the same every month. Default categories: Housing, Utilities, Insurance, Subscriptions.

Group 2 — Everyday Spending (sort 2): Regular variable spending that happens most weeks. Default categories: Groceries, Dining, Transportation, Personal Care.

Group 3 — Irregular / True Expenses (sort 3): Predictable but infrequent — plan for these monthly even when they do not hit every month (the YNAB true-expenses concept). Default categories: Household, Healthcare, Travel, Gifts, Education, Shopping.

Group 4 — Savings & Goals (sort 4): Money leaving the spending pool for a future purpose. Default categories: Savings.

Group 5 — Just for Fun (sort 5): Discretionary and lifestyle spending. Default categories: Entertainment, Gaming, Vices.

Group 6 — Income (sort 6): Inflows; displayed separately from spending groups. Default categories: Income.
steps: Navigate to /budget → Categories
Create a group named 'Fixed Monthly'
Drag or assign Housing, Insurance, Subscriptions into that group
Verify the category list renders the group header with categories nested beneath it
Collapse the group and verify the categories hide
Verify uncategorized categories appear under a default 'Other' group
Verify the analytics page respects group order when rendering categories

A category is 'over budget' when actual spending exceeds the target; the delta is highlighted in red. Targets are stored per-category (not per-month) as a default monthly amount, with optional per-month overrides in a budget_targets table keyed by (category_id, year, month).

features.title: Ready to assign — unallocated balance indicator
features.description: Show a persistent 'Ready to assign' figure at the top of the budget page: total income for the month minus the sum of all category budget targets. When positive, the user still has dollars without a job. When zero, every dollar is assigned. When negative, they have over-allocated. This is the forcing function for zero-based budgeting — it should be the most prominent number on the page.

features.title: Month-to-month rollover for underspent categories

If Groceries is budgeted at $500 and only $380 is spent, the following month starts with a $120 carry-forward added to the $500 target. Rollover is opt-in per category (a boolean flag). Overspending in a rollover category reduces the following month's effective budget rather than resetting to zero.

features.title: Workout data model refactor — first-class sets, exercise library, superset groups
features.description:
Foundation for all subsequent workout features. The existing model likely stores sets as JSONB on the exercise row; this refactor promotes sets to first-class rows and introduces an exercise library. Audit the current workout domain (api/src/life_dashboard/domains/workouts/) before starting — preserve any passing behaviour.

Target schema:
- exercises: id, name, muscle_groups[] (JSONB), equipment_type, tracking_type ('reps'|'duration'|'distance'), is_global (bool — pre-seeded library entries), created_by_user_id (nullable for global). Seed ~60 common exercises on migration.
- workout_templates: id, user_id, name, description, estimated_duration_minutes, last_used_at (nullable timestamp).
- template_exercises: id, template_id, exercise_id, position (int), superset_group_id (nullable UUID — same UUID = same superset, max 5 exercises per group), default_sets (int), default_reps (nullable int), default_weight (nullable decimal), default_rest_seconds (nullable int), notes (nullable text).
- workout_sessions: id, user_id, template_id (nullable FK), name, started_at, ended_at (nullable), notes (nullable).
- session_exercises: id, session_id, exercise_id, template_exercise_id (nullable FK for pre-load), position (int), superset_group_id (nullable UUID), notes (nullable).
- workout_sets: id, session_exercise_id, set_number (int), reps (nullable int), target_reps (nullable int — planned reps, for detecting failures), duration_seconds (nullable int — for timed exercises), weight (nullable decimal), weight_unit ('lbs'|'kg'), distance_meters (nullable decimal — for cardio exercises like running/cycling/rowing), distance_unit ('km'|'mi', nullable), rest_seconds (nullable int), is_warmup (bool default false), rpe (nullable int 1–10), completed_at (nullable timestamp).

User settings: add prompt_save_as_template boolean (default true) to control the post-session save prompt.

Superset rules enforced at the service layer: a superset_group_id must have 2–5 members within the same template or session; attempting to add a 6th returns 400. When an exercise is removed from a group that would leave only 1 member, clear superset_group_id from the remaining member too.
steps: Audit existing workout models — document what already exists and what needs migration
Run migration — verify all six new/updated tables exist with correct columns and indexes
Seed global exercise library — verify ~60 exercises are present with correct muscle_groups and tracking_type
Create a template exercise with superset_group_id shared with a second exercise — verify both are returned in the same group from GET /workouts/templates/:id
Attempt to add a 6th exercise to a superset group — verify 400 response
Remove one exercise from a 2-member superset group — verify the remaining exercise's superset_group_id is cleared
Remove one exercise from a 3-member superset group — verify the remaining two retain their superset_group_id

Verify prompt_save_as_template exists on user/member settings with default true

features.title: Budget profiles — foundation (data model + account assignment)
features.description:
Introduce budget_profiles as a named financial context sitting above the existing personal/household scope. Each household gets two default profiles seeded on creation: 'Personal' and 'Household'. Add profile_id FK to budget_accounts, budget_categories, budget_category_groups, and budget_targets. Backfill existing data: accounts/categories with scope='personal' → Personal profile; scope='shared'/'household' → Household profile. Add a profile switcher dropdown to the budget page header. All existing budget functionality continues to work — this phase is purely additive and invisible to users except for the profile switcher UI.

Key design decisions:
- budget_profiles: (id, household_id, name, budgeting_style ['zero_based'|'profit_tracking'], currency, sort_order, created_at)
- Accounts are assigned to exactly one profile (no many-to-many); cross-profile expenses handled via per-transaction override (budget-011)
- Profile switcher defaults to the last-used profile (stored in localStorage)
- 'Personal' and 'Household' profiles are always present and cannot be deleted
steps: Run migration — verify budget_profiles table exists with Personal and Household rows for the household
Verify all existing accounts have a profile_id pointing at the correct default profile
Verify all existing categories have a profile_id
Open /budget — verify profile switcher dropdown appears in the header

features.title: Budget profiles — scope/profile separation (data model cleanup)
features.description:
Cleanly decouple 'scope' (visibility within a profile) from 'profile' (financial context/attribution). Currently the scope column on transactions conflates both concepts. After profiles exist, scope should mean only: is this transaction private to the owner, or shared with all profile members? Attribution is handled by profile_id (via the account).

Migration: for existing transactions, scope='household' → scope='shared' + profile=Household profile; scope='personal' → scope='private' + profile=Personal profile. Rename scope enum values from 'personal'/'household' to 'private'/'shared' to remove the naming ambiguity. Update all service layer queries, schemas, and frontend references accordingly.
steps: Run migration — verify existing transactions have correct scope and profile_id after backfill
Verify household-scoped transactions (now scope='shared') are visible to all profile members
Verify personal-scoped transactions (now scope='private') are visible only to the owner
Verify expense splitting still works correctly for shared transactions in the Household profile
Verify the API returns the updated scope values and the frontend renders them correctly
Verify no existing split_config or split_override data was lost

features.title: Budget profiles — cross-profile transaction re-attribution
features.description: Handle the common case where a transaction imported into one profile actually belongs in another (e.g. Alice pays a shared grocery run on her personal card, but the expense belongs in the Household profile). Add a nullable profile_id override column to budget_transactions. When set, it overrides the account's profile for analytics, categorization, and visibility — the transaction physically stays linked to its account (for balance tracking) but analytically lives in the target profile.
steps: Import a transaction into a Personal profile account
Open the transaction and use 'Move to profile' to re-attribute it to the Household profile
Verify the transaction appears in Household profile analytics but not Personal profile analytics
Verify the source account balance is unaffected
If the target category has a split_config, verify the split prompt appears

features.title: Budget profiles — Business profile type with profit-tracking analytics
features.description:
Add a 'Business' profile option with budgeting_style='profit_tracking'. This style presents a P&L view instead of envelope budgeting: revenue streams vs. operating expenses, net profit figure, and a rolling trend chart. The 'Ready to assign' banner is replaced by a 'Net profit' figure (revenue minus expenses). Category groups are pre-seeded for a business context: Revenue, Cost of Goods, Operating Expenses, Owner's Pay.

Creating a Business profile is a paid-tier feature — enforce at the service layer by checking profile count against the household's subscription tier before allowing creation.
steps: Create a new Business profile (paid tier required) — verify it appears in the profile switcher
Verify Business profile shows P&L view: revenue vs expenses, net profit figure
Verify the 'Ready to assign' banner is absent; 'Net profit' shown instead
Add income transactions and expense transactions — verify they appear in correct categories
Verify a rolling 12-month trend chart renders with revenue and expense lines
Attempt to create a Business profile on a free-tier account — verify it is blocked with an upgrade prompt
Verify envelope budgeting (zero_based) still works correctly on Personal and Household profiles

features.title: Budget profiles — MRR tracking for profit-tracking profiles
features.description: For Business profiles, allow income transactions and categories to be flagged as recurring revenue (MRR). Add a boolean is_recurring_revenue flag to budget_categories (profit_tracking profiles only). When set, the analytics layer aggregates those transactions separately and reports: actual MRR (sum of recurring income this month), projected ARR (MRR × 12), and month-over-month MRR growth.
steps: Open a Business profile income category and enable 'Recurring revenue (MRR)'
Add monthly recurring income transactions to that category
Verify the Business profile dashboard shows: actual MRR, projected ARR, MoM growth
Add a second recurring revenue category and verify MRR totals aggregate correctly
Navigate to a month with no recurring income — verify MRR shows $0 not null
Disable recurring revenue on a category and verify it drops out of MRR aggregate

features.title: Budget profiles — profile member access control
features.description: Add explicit per-profile membership with roles, replacing the implicit 'all household members see everything' default. Add a budget_profile_members table: (profile_id, user_id, role ['owner'|'member'|'viewer']). Profiles default to including all household members as 'member' on creation (preserving existing behaviour). Owners can restrict a profile to a subset of household members.

Remove one member from the Business profile

features.title: Transaction search
features.description: Add a search box above the transaction list that filters by description and merchant name (case-insensitive substring match). Also add an 'Uncategorized only' toggle. Both filters work together and reset pagination when changed.

features.title: Spending trends chart
features.description: Add a spending trends view to the budget analytics section. Shows the last N calendar months as a grouped bar chart: income (green), expenses (red), budgeted (sky). Hovering a month shows detail. Toggled via a TrendingUp button in the analytics view switcher.

Verify the chart shows the last 6 months with income / expense / budgeted bars
Hover a month bar group — verify the detail tooltip shows correct figures

features.title: Account balance field
features.description: Add a manually-maintained current_balance field to budget_accounts. Users enter the balance from their bank statement. Balance is displayed in the account dropdown list and as a clickable chip below the account selector when a specific account is selected. Clicking the chip shows an inline input to update the balance; saving auto-stamps balance_updated_at.
steps: Select a specific account from the dropdown
Verify a 'Set balance' chip appears below the account selector
Click the chip — verify an inline input appears

features.title: Income forecasting — assign next month's money before it arrives
features.description: A forward-looking monthly allocation view inspired by YNAB's 'Assign Money' workflow. Unlike the current 'Ready to assign' banner (budget-007), which is reactive (actual income received minus targets), income forecasting is proactive: it shows projected income for an upcoming or current month based on recurring income transactions (budget-004), then lets the user allocate that projected amount to envelope categories before transactions arrive.
steps: Navigate to /budget and select a future or current month
Click the 'Plan' or 'Forecast' view toggle
Verify projected income is shown based on recurring income transactions for that month
Verify 'Left to allocate' = projected income minus sum of category targets
Adjust a category target inline and verify 'Left to allocate' updates immediately

Over-allocate and verify a negative / warning state
Navigate to a past month and verify projected income is replaced by actual income
Verify months with no recurring income show $0 projected (not null or error)

features.title: Teller bank linking — connect real bank accounts
features.description: Integrate Teller (teller.io) as the bank connectivity layer for importing transactions and syncing account balances. Teller is developer-friendly with a free tier up to 100 connections, scaling per-connection beyond that — well-suited for self-hosted and early cloud tiers. On the backend, store encrypted Teller enrollment_id and access_token per budget_account. Expose a /budget/accounts/:id/link endpoint that initiates the Teller Connect flow and exchanges the one-time token for a persistent access_token. A background sync job polls Teller for new transactions and upserts them into budget_transactions (deduplicated by teller_transaction_id). Account balances returned by Teller overwrite the manual current_balance field (budget-017) when a sync runs.
steps: Navigate to Budget → Accounts and click 'Link account'
Verify the Teller Connect modal opens
Complete the bank auth flow for a supported test institution
Verify a new budget_account is created with the Teller enrollment linked
Verify transactions from the past 30 days are imported and categorized

features.title: Coinbase crypto portfolio — live asset values (paid tier)
features.description: Allow users to connect a Coinbase account via OAuth and display their crypto holdings with live USD valuations. This is a paid-tier feature — enforce at the service layer before initiating the OAuth flow.

features.title: Goal financial linking — spending_cap syncs with budget category
features.description: Goals can be linked to a budget category via financial_link.type=spending_cap. Creating or updating a goal with this link syncs monthly_limit → BudgetCategory.default_monthly_amount. The AI set_category_budget tool syncs back to linked goals. After each Teller sync, check_budget_thresholds fires household notifications at 80% and 100% of any category's monthly target (once per threshold per month).
steps: Create a goal with financial_link={type: spending_cap, category_id: <uuid>, monthly_limit: 500}
Verify the linked BudgetCategory.default_monthly_amount is set to 500
Ask the AI agent to change the dining budget to $400

Spend over 100%; verify a budget_threshold_100 notification fires

features.title: User profile + bootstrap pass (Phase 1 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: A persistent, per-user markdown profile that captures who the user is — current focuses, values, recurring patterns, what drains/works, things to not bring up. Stored on the existing member_ai_memory.memory_text column (reused rather than introducing a new user_profiles table — the chatbot was already reading it). The new bits: (1) last_bootstrapped_at column on member_ai_memory so we can tell whether the richer bootstrap pass has run; (2) a new user_profile_updates table holding proposed AI changes that the user accepts or rejects — the AI never silently rewrites the profile via this surface. (3) A bootstrap pass (POST /ai/profile/bootstrap) reads the user's notes, documents, active goals/projects/habits, and the last 90 days of completed todos to draft an initial profile. (4) The coach now loads the profile into its system prompt alongside the existing behavioral context; the chatbot already read memory_text so it benefits automatically. (5) A new 'Profile' subsection in Settings → AI: view current profile, edit directly, run bootstrap, review/accept/reject pending proposals. See docs/ai-coach-redesign.md for the full design.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head; verify last_bootstrapped_at exists on member_ai_memory and user_profile_updates table exists with the documented columns
Navigate to Settings → AI and find the new 'Profile' subsection at the top — for an existing user with empty memory_text it shows 'Your profile is empty' with a 'Build my profile' button
Click 'Build my profile' — bootstrap pass runs, creates a pending user_profile_update with source=bootstrap; the pending card appears below the empty state
Review the proposed profile content; accept it
Verify member_ai_memory.memory_text is populated and last_updated_at advances; the Profile section now renders the accepted content

Trigger a coach digest (POST /ai/coach/digest/generate) — verify the digest reads naturally as if it knows the user (the system prompt now includes a 'What you know about this person' fragment with the profile content)
Open the chat surface and start a new conversation — verify the response is grounded in the profile (e.g. references a current focus or stated preference without name-dropping the profile)
Edit the profile directly via the UI textarea; verify the edit persists (PATCH /ai/profile) and shows in the next coach run
Bootstrap again to create a second pending proposal, then reject it — verify status flips to rejected (POST /ai/profile/updates/{id}/reject returns 204) and memory_text is unchanged
Bootstrap a third time, then accept one of two pending proposals — verify the other still-pending one is automatically marked 'superseded' (so the user never accepts a stale-base proposal by mistake)

features.title: Notes-driven incremental profile proposer (Phase 1.5 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Closes the 'profile goes stale between bootstrap clicks' gap. After every N net-new notes (default 5) created or updated by a user, a background task runs the same proposer prompt as bootstrap but with a smaller, fresher source bundle (the last 15 notes). It either creates a pending UserProfileUpdate (source='incremental') or returns SKIP silently — the system prompt biases hard toward SKIP. Counter `notes_at_last_proposal` lives on member_ai_memory and advances even when the proposer skips, so each user can never trigger more than one proposer call per N new notes. Skips entirely until bootstrap has run at least once.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head; verify notes_at_last_proposal exists on member_ai_memory
As a user who has NOT yet run bootstrap, create 6 new notes; verify NO UserProfileUpdate rows are created (incremental skips until bootstrap has run)
Run bootstrap and accept the proposal; profile is now populated
Create 4 new notes; verify NO proposer call yet (under threshold)
Create the 5th new note; verify a background proposer call fires (logs show 'Incremental proposer' messages) and notes_at_last_proposal advances to the new total
If the proposer found durable signal: a pending UserProfileUpdate with source='incremental' appears in Settings → AI → Profile
If the proposer returned SKIP: no new pending update appears, but notes_at_last_proposal still advanced — verify by adding 5 more notes and watching for the next proposer run
Accept the incremental proposal; verify memory_text is replaced and last_updated_at advances
Verify a note write that fails the proposer call (e.g. AI provider misconfigured) does NOT block the note save — the note commit completes, errors are logged

features.title: Journal signal extraction (Phase 2 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Per-entry structured signal extraction for journal notes. Adds a 'kind' column to collections (kind='journal' identifies journal collections). When a note in a journal-kind collection is saved AND the user has ai_journal_extraction_enabled=true AND a provider is configured, a background asyncio task extracts {sentiment in [-1,+1], self_talk_valence (positive/neutral/harsh/mixed), themes[], notable_phrases[], energy_level, entry_date, extraction_version} and upserts a journal_signals row keyed by note_id. Coach trend helpers (sentiment_trend, harsh_self_talk_streak, dominant_themes_recent) live in journal_signal_service for Phase 3 to use.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head (Postgres) OR restart the API on SQLite; verify journal_signals table exists, collection.kind exists, ai_settings.ai_journal_extraction_enabled exists (default true)

Check the boot log for 'Journal-kind backfill: tagged=X, seeded=Y, already-tagged=Z' — verify every existing household ends up with at least one collection where kind='journal' after the startup hook runs
Sign up a brand-new account; verify the new household gets a Journal collection seeded automatically via the auth/setup flow
In Settings → AI → Journal signals, verify the toggle reads 'On' and the 'Backfill existing entries' button is enabled
Create a new note inside the Journal collection with a meaningful body; verify a journal_signals row appears with non-null sentiment, valence, entry_date, extraction_version=1
Edit that note's content_md; verify the signals row is REPLACED (still one row per note_id, extracted_at advances)
Edit only the title; verify the signals row is NOT re-extracted (title-only changes are skipped)
Run POST /ai/journal-signals/backfill; verify the response shows scanned/extracted/skipped_empty/skipped_current/errors counts
Toggle 'Extract signals on save' off; create another journal entry; verify NO new signals row is created
Toggle back on; save the same entry again and verify extraction resumes
Delete a journal note; verify the journal_signals row cascades and is removed
Confirm a non-journal-collection note does NOT trigger extraction
Restart the API a second time; confirm the backfill log line shows tagged=0 seeded=0 (idempotent)

features.title: CBT-aware coach prompt rewrite (Phase 3 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Coach prompts rewritten to reason across all three context layers (profile, narrative signals, raw recent entries) plus existing behavioral data. New _fetch_narrative_context helper pulls sentiment trend, harsh-self-talk streak, dominant themes, and the last 5 journal entries verbatim. New _COACH_METHOD_PROMPT system-prompt fragment defines the CBT moves: reality-test harsh self-talk against strong-week data, be honest (not flattering) during real dips, 'bird by bird' register during double-negatives, no manufactured lessons during double-positives, no name-dropping the profile, journal quoting capped at one per response.
steps: Generate a morning digest in a state where: (a) the last week's todos show a strong completion record AND (b) recent journal entries are harshly self-critical. Verify the coach reality-tests the self-criticism against the data
Generate an evening digest after a real productivity dip + journal entries that sound confident or upbeat. Verify the coach is honest and grounded, not flattering
Generate a digest in a 'both-negative' state (low completion + run of negative journal entries). Verify the response is brief and offers at most one next step
Generate a digest in a 'both-positive' state. Verify recognition is proportionate — no manufactured lessons
Run digests across all four tones (supportive, stoic, drill_sergeant, gentle_mentor) with the same data. Verify the CBT method is consistent and only the voice changes
Inspect the assembled system prompt — verify order: tone voice → '## How to coach' → method prompt → profile fragment
Inspect the user message — verify it contains a '## Recent narrative' section with sentiment numbers, themes, and recent journal entries
Verify that when the user has NO journal entries, the narrative section is omitted entirely
Verify the coach never name-drops the profile across 10 sample runs
Verify the coach quotes at most one short journal phrase per response across 10 sample runs

features.title: Context-aware chatbot — read the currently open resource
features.description: The sidebar chatbot now knows what resource the user is currently viewing (note, recipe, document, todo, goal, habit) and can answer questions about 'this' without the user pasting content. New optional `context: {type, id}` field on ChatRequest. Server-side chat_context_resolver dispatches by type, enforces visibility/ownership, and produces a 'What the user is currently viewing' markdown block prepended to the system prompt. Frontend: new CurrentResourceProvider, useRegisterCurrentResource hook, 'Discussing: <title>' chip above the chat textarea.
steps: Open a note in the Journal collection; verify the chat sidebar shows 'Discussing: <note title>' chip
Ask the chatbot 'looking at this entry, am I being too hard on myself?' — verify the response is specific to that entry's content
Open a recipe; verify the chip updates to 'Discussing: <recipe name>'
Ask 'what could I swap for a spicier version?' — verify the response references the recipe's actual ingredients
Open a document; verify the chip and that the AI knows the document's title/description
Navigate to /todos or /habits (no [id] page); verify the chip disappears and the chatbot reverts to general behavior
Try opening another user's note via a manipulated request — verify the response treats it as if no context was attached (silent failure)

Verify the OpenAPI client regenerates cleanly and the frontend tsc check stays at 0 errors

features.title: Coach widget free-text focus field
features.description: Lets the user write 1-2 sentences of free-text guidance for what the coach should focus on, persisted per-widget in AiCoachWidgetConfig.focus and threaded through to /ai/coach/digest/generate. Goes beyond the structured pinned-projects/goals/habits selectors: 'I'm preparing for a hard conversation today' or 'I want to recover from a rough weekend'. Server renders a '## What I want you to focus on right now' section in the user message just before the briefing instructions.
steps: Open the AI coach widget settings; find the new 'Focus for this coach' textarea below the pinned-habit selectors
Type a focus string and trigger Generate / Regenerate
Verify the digest response acknowledges the focus and shapes its content around it

Confirm focus is included in the prompt only when non-empty

features.title: Silent profile + auto-bootstrap on API key save + key validation
features.description: Three connected UX changes that turn the AI profile from a user-facing surface into a quiet background service. (1) ProfileSubSection removed from Settings → AI; profile is no longer visible or editable to end users (backend /ai/profile/* endpoints remain for debugging). (2) Bootstrap pass and the notes-driven incremental proposer now write directly to member_ai_memory.memory_text instead of queueing pending updates. (3) AIProvider gains a validate() method; AnthropicProvider implements it via a max_tokens=1 probe call. PATCH /ai/settings calls validate() before persisting a new key — bad keys return 400. (4) On a successful new-key save, /ai/settings fires the bootstrap pass in a background asyncio task if the user has no profile yet.
steps: In Settings → AI, verify there is NO 'Profile' subsection
Save a deliberately invalid Anthropic API key — verify the request returns 400 with a clear message and that ai_settings.api_key_encrypted is NOT updated
Save a valid Anthropic API key — verify the request succeeds, the key is persisted, and within a few seconds the API log shows the bootstrap pass running
Wait for the bootstrap task to complete; query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify it now contains a populated profile
Trigger a coach digest — verify the response is grounded in the auto-bootstrapped profile
Open the chat sidebar and start a new conversation — verify the assistant references the profile content
Save the SAME key a second time when memory_text is already populated — verify no second bootstrap fires
Write 5+ new journal entries; verify the incremental proposer auto-applies updates directly to memory_text
Verify that clearing the key via clear_api_key=true does NOT delete memory_text

features.title: Chat-driven profile updates via update_profile tool + coach widget combobox
features.description: Two changes that make the chatbot a first-class profile maintainer and tame the coach widget UI when users have many projects/habits. (1) New `update_profile` chat tool: the AI calls it silently when the user says things like 'focus more on consistency,' 'I've been drinking too much,' 'stop bringing up the old job.' The tool takes a content_md string and a diff_summary, writes directly to member_ai_memory.memory_text, and records an audit row. (2) PinnedSection in the AI coach widget switches to a searchable combobox + chip-list above 8 items per category.
steps: Open the chat sidebar and say 'I want to focus more on consistency and showing up daily moving forward.' Verify the assistant acknowledges in plain language WITHOUT mentioning the tool or the profile
Query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify the profile has been updated
Verify a row exists in user_profile_updates with source='manual', status='accepted'
Trigger a fresh coach digest — verify the response reflects the new focus
Say something transient like 'I had a bad day at work today.' Verify the assistant responds empathetically but does NOT call update_profile
Configure a coach widget with FEW projects/goals/habits (≤8 of each) — verify the chip-grid layout
Configure a coach widget with MANY projects (>8) — verify the combobox appears
Click the 'Pick from N' button; verify the popover opens with autofocused search input and filtered scrollable list
Click outside the popover; verify it closes

features.title: Profile versioning + weekly scheduled refresh + decay (Phase 4)
features.description: Final phase of the AI coach redesign. Three connected pieces. (1) Migration adds user_profile_versions append-only history table. A new _apply_profile_update helper is the single write path for memory_text — all write sites flow through it, snapshotting old content before overwrite, trimming rows beyond PROFILE_VERSION_RETENTION (50). (2) New run_scheduled_profile_refresh function: integrate durable patterns from last 4 weeks AND decay stale content (things that haven't recurred, resolved past situations, fixed-trait language). (3) New APScheduler weekly job at Sunday 03:00 iterates every user with a non-empty profile. (4) GET /ai/profile/versions?limit=N for debug.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head; verify user_profile_versions table exists
Save a new API key to trigger bootstrap; query user_profile_versions — verify NO row was created (snapshotting empty old content is correctly skipped)
After bootstrap, edit the profile via PATCH /ai/profile; query user_profile_versions — verify ONE row exists with source='direct_edit' and the OLD bootstrap content_md
Trigger the update_profile chat tool; verify a SECOND version row appears with source='manual'
Call GET /ai/profile/versions?limit=10 — verify it returns history newest-first
Restart the API; verify the boot log shows the scheduler started with 'profile_refresh=Sun 03:00'
Manually invoke run_scheduled_profile_refresh_all() from a Python shell — verify it logs {users, applied, skip, error} counts
Insert 60 mock version rows for a user; trigger another _apply_profile_update; verify count is trimmed back to 50
Confirm the scheduled refresh prompt biases toward SKIP when the profile is healthy and there's minimal new activity

features.title: Guided journaling tool — Talk-it-out sessions with personalized openers
features.description: Adds a 'Talk it out' button to today's journal entry that launches a full-screen guided journal session. Adaptive AI register: matches the user's energy — rant mode gets brief acknowledgements and no questions, reflective mode gets focused questions, never starts with a question, never gives advice unsolicited. On Finish, the AI synthesizes a first-person summary; user reviews/edits, then saves. Optional 'Save with transcript' toggle appends the full conversation below a `---` divider. The saved content appends to today's existing journal entry.
steps: Restart the API — confirm ai_conversations has kind + note_id columns
Open today's journal entry — verify the 'Talk it out' button appears
Open YESTERDAY's journal entry — verify the button does NOT appear
Click 'Talk it out' on today's entry — full-screen overlay opens and the AI's opener appears
Send a message; verify the AI streams back a brief, non-question response
Test rant register: vent for 2-3 messages; verify the AI keeps responses short and doesn't pile on questions
Test reflective register: say something planning-oriented; verify the AI shifts to focused-question mode
Click 'Finish'; verify the AI synthesizes a first-person summary in an editable textarea
Toggle OFF 'Save with transcript'; click Save — verify the journal note has the summary appended
Re-open the same day's entry and click 'Talk it out' again — verify the session RESUMES

features.title: Talk-it-out check-in modes (Mood, Body, Rant, Day review)
features.description: Added pre-built check-in modes the user picks before the conversation begins. Modes: Mood check, Body check (somatic anchor), Rant (vent — system prompt tells the AI to stay with the user, NOT reality-test), Day review (looks AHEAD before 4pm / BACK after 4pm). Each mode seeds a canned opening message (zero latency) and appends a mode-specific instruction block to the journal system prompt. Mode is locked once chosen.
steps: Restart the API
Open Talk-it-out on today's journal entry — verify the 4 structured mode chips appear
Click 'Mood check' — verify the canned opener appears within ~200ms and the picker disappears
Type a reply — verify the AI's response stays short and feeling-focused
Pick 'Rant' — type a complaint and verify the AI stays with you (no reality-test, no reframe)
Pick 'Day review' in the morning — verify the opener asks 'Looking ahead at today...'
Pick 'Day review' in the evening — verify the opener asks 'What's standing out from today...'
Click 'blank slate' link — verify the picker disappears with no opener
Close the modal AFTER picking a mode and typing something — verify reopening goes straight to the conversation

features.title: Rate limiting on auth endpoints
features.description: Brute-force and spam protection on public auth endpoints using slowapi (in-memory storage, single-process). Limits: POST /auth/login — 5/minute per IP; POST /auth/register — 3/hour per IP. Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header.
steps: Start the API and send 6 rapid POST /auth/login requests from the same IP
Verify the 6th request returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests
Verify the response includes a Retry-After header
Wait 1 minute and verify login works again
Send 4 POST /auth/register requests within an hour
Verify the 4th returns HTTP 429
Verify legitimate login from a different IP is unaffected during a lockout

features.title: Encryption at rest — managed Postgres (cloud deployment checklist)
features.description: When moving to a managed Postgres provider (Neon, Supabase, AWS RDS, etc.), enable encryption at rest as a provider-level checkbox. This is not a code change — it's a deploy-time configuration step. Protects against physical disk theft and raw DB dumps. All managed providers support it; it should be verified as enabled on day one of any cloud deployment.
steps: Confirm encryption at rest is enabled in the managed Postgres provider's settings before accepting any real user data
Document the setting location in infra/CLAUDE.md for the cloud deployment tier
Verify SSL/TLS is enforced for all connections to the DB

features.title: Field-level encryption for sensitive database columns (Phase 1 + 2)
features.description: Fernet-based (AES-128-CBC + HMAC-SHA256) field-level encryption via SQLAlchemy TypeDecorators that transparently encrypt on write and decrypt on read at the ORM boundary. Phase 1: BudgetAccount.teller_access_token, AiSettings.api_key_encrypted. Phase 2: MemberAiMemory.memory_text, AiCoachDigest.content, UserProfileUpdate.proposed_content_md, UserProfileVersion.content_md, JournalSignal.notable_phrases. Key managed via FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY env var; comma-separated keys supported for zero-downtime rotation via MultiFernet.
steps: Verify FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY is set in api/.env
Run make migrate — confirm 0036 applies cleanly
Save a BYOK API key; confirm AI chat still works
Link a bank account via Teller; confirm sync still works
Directly query the DB — verify the stored api_key_encrypted value is a Fernet ciphertext (starts with 'gAAAAA')
Directly query journal_signals.notable_phrases — verify stored values are Fernet ciphertext
Directly query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify stored value is ciphertext
Restart the API — verify no errors on boot; encrypted values still decrypt correctly
Test key rotation: prepend a new Fernet key to FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY; restart; verify existing values still decrypt

features.title: Zero-knowledge / E2EE opt-in for cloud tier
features.description: Opt-in end-to-end encryption for users who want the strongest privacy guarantee. When enabled, the most sensitive fields (AI profile, journal content) are encrypted client-side before upload using a key derived from the user's password (Argon2 → symmetric key). The server stores only ciphertext it cannot read. Trade-offs: password recovery requires saved recovery codes; server-side AI features cannot operate on E2EE fields without client-side decryption first; household shared data requires a household key encrypted with each member's derived key.
steps: During onboarding, verify a privacy mode selection step appears with clear Standard vs Private mode explanation
Selecting Private mode triggers recovery code generation and download
User must confirm they have saved recovery codes before proceeding
Verify AI coach features show a 'requires decryption' prompt when E2EE is active
Verify forgot-password flow is replaced by a recovery code flow for E2EE users
Verify a second household member can read shared data encrypted with the household key
Verify toggling E2EE on in Settings re-encrypts existing data client-side

features.title: Field-level encryption Phase 3 — evaluate notes, messages, transactions, contacts
features.description: Decision point: evaluate whether and how to encrypt the remaining high-volume content fields. Fields in scope: notes.content_md + notes.content_json (journal/note bodies), ai_messages.content (chat history), budget_transactions.description (raw bank descriptions), contacts PII (email, phone, address). The blocker is search: these fields either have active full-text search or LIKE-based filtering, and naive Fernet encryption breaks both. Must be co-designed with search-001 architecture.

features.title: Transaction data retention — purge raw transactions, keep monthly aggregates
features.description: Data minimization: raw bank transaction descriptions are sensitive and don't need to be kept forever. After a configurable retention window (default 24 months), purge raw BudgetTransaction rows and keep only monthly category aggregates in a new BudgetMonthlySummary model. A user-configurable retention_months field on BudgetProfile (default 24, options: 12/24/60/forever). Periodic background job (APScheduler, requires scheduler-001) runs the purge on a schedule.

features.title: Per-member AI access control

Backend: ai_features_enabled boolean on household_memberships; require_ai_enabled FastAPI dependency raises 403 on all 22 /ai/* endpoints when disabled; new PATCH /households/members/{user_id} for admins (cannot disable their own AI through this endpoint). features.title: Workout template builder UX — exercise library, superset linking, drag-to-reorder
features.description:
The template editing interface where users pre-build workouts before arriving at the gym. Requires workouts-001 (data model) to be complete first.

Exercise list in a template:
- Each exercise row shows: 3-dot handle | exercise name | target sets summary | → (tap to drill into detail).
- Standalone exercises and collapsed superset rows are both first-class items in the list.
- A collapsed superset row renders as 'Exercise A + Exercise B' (or 'Exercise A + 2 others' for 3+). It has one 3-dot handle, one tap target, and counts as one draggable item.

3-dot handle behaviour:
- Single tap: opens a context menu containing: Edit exercise settings, Link as superset, Unlink superset (only when in a superset), Add note, Remove exercise.
- Tap-and-hold: activates drag-to-reorder for that row. Dragging a collapsed superset row moves the entire group as a unit.

Superset link mode (entered via 3-dot → 'Link as superset'):
- All 3-dot icons become checkboxes. The tapped exercise is pre-checked.
- User taps additional exercises (standalone or collapsed supersets) to select them.
- Fixed bottom bar shows [Cancel] and [Link] (disabled until 2+ items selected). Both buttons on the same line.
- Tapping a collapsed superset row selects all its member exercises.
- Tapping [Link]: all selected exercises merge into one superset group, capped at 5 exercises (6th selection is blocked with a toast). Non-adjacent selections are reordered to sit together at the position of the highest-ranked exercise in the selection. Edit mode exits and the list returns to 3-dot mode.
- Tapping [Cancel]: exits edit mode, nothing changes.

Unlinking supersets:
- 3-dot on a collapsed superset row → 'Unlink superset'. Atomic: dissolves the entire group, all exercises return to standalone rows at the position the superset occupied, in their previous internal order.
- No partial removal from a superset. To change membership: unlink all, re-link the desired subset.

Reordering within a superset:
- Cannot be done from the main template list (the superset is a single collapsed row).
- Internal order is managed on the superset's detail page, which has its own per-exercise drag handles.
- Dragging exercise A below exercise B within the detail page swaps their order but keeps both in the superset.

Exercise picker (adding exercises to a template):
- Search input (searches exercise name). Results sorted by last_used_at DESC so recently used exercises surface first.
- Two sections: 'My exercises' (user-created) and 'Exercise library' (global seed). Both searchable.
- Tap an exercise to add it to the bottom of the template list.
- '+' button to create a new exercise inline (name, muscle group, tracking type).

Template list (the /workouts/templates route):
- Sorted by last_used_at DESC.
- Each row: template name, exercise count, last used date (e.g. '3 days ago').
- Search input to filter by name.
steps: Navigate to /workouts/templates and verify templates are sorted by last_used_at DESC with a search input
Create a new template and add exercises from the library — verify results sort by last_used_at with search working
Tap 3-dot on an exercise → verify the context menu appears with all expected options
Tap-and-hold a 3-dot handle and drag the row — verify the exercise reorders in the list
Tap 3-dot → 'Link as superset' — verify all 3-dots become checkboxes and the tapped exercise is pre-checked
Select a second non-adjacent exercise and tap [Link] — verify both exercises collapse into one row at the higher position, displayed as 'Exercise A + Exercise B'
Attempt to add a 6th exercise to a superset in link mode — verify the 6th tap is blocked with a toast
Tap-and-hold the collapsed superset row — verify dragging moves both exercises together as a unit
Tap the collapsed superset row → detail page — verify internal order can be changed via drag handles on the detail page
3-dot on superset → 'Unlink superset' — verify both exercises return as standalone rows at the superset's former position
Add a note to an exercise via the 3-dot menu — verify it appears as a muted line below the exercise name in the list

features.title: Workout session logging UX — live logging, ghost values, rest timer, save-as-template prompt
features.description:
The active session experience: starting a workout, logging sets in real time, and wrapping up. Requires workouts-001 (data model) and workouts-002 (templates) to be complete first.

Starting a session:
- 'New workout' button on the workouts page. Options: pick a template (list sorted by last_used_at DESC, with search), or start blank.
- Starting from a template: session opens with all template exercises pre-loaded as session_exercises. Ghost values (muted, slightly transparent) pre-fill each set's weight and reps fields from the most recent session that used the same template_exercise_id. If no prior session exists for that template, fields are empty.
- Starting blank: empty session, user adds exercises via the same exercise picker as the template builder.

Session overview (the main list view):
- One row per exercise (standalone or collapsed superset, same visual rules as the template builder).
- Each row shows: exercise name, target sets from template (e.g. '3×8'), and a completion indicator (e.g. '✓ 2/3 sets').
- Tap anywhere on a row (except the 3-dot handle) to open the exercise detail page.
- A persistent header shows session duration (live timer) and a 'Finish workout' button.

Exercise detail page:
- Shows a table of sets: set number | [weight input] × [reps input] | ✓ button.
- Ghost values (muted style) pre-populate weight and reps from the previous session for the same exercise. Tapping any field activates it and clears the ghost state for that field.
- Warmup sets (is_warmup=true) are displayed in a smaller, muted style above the working sets with a 'W' badge. They do not count toward the completion indicator on the overview.
- A 'W+ Add warmup set' button above the working sets; '+ Add set' button at the bottom copies the last set's values as a starting point.
- Tapping ✓ on a set marks it complete (completed_at = now), triggers the rest timer, and advances the highlighted row to the next set.
- Rest timer: displayed at the bottom of the detail page. Starts automatically when a set is marked complete. Shows countdown from the template's default_rest_seconds (or a fallback of 90s). User can tap to dismiss or adjust. Timer persists if the user navigates back to the overview and returns.
- Optional rpe field per set (shown as a subtle 1–10 input below the reps field, collapsed by default; a 'Show RPE' toggle in session settings enables it).
- Target_reps field: shown as a muted reference next to the reps input if set (e.g. '— target: 8'). Reps logged vs. target_reps feeds into the progress chart failure detection.

Superset detail page:
- Shows both (or all) exercises in the group together, interleaved by round.
- ROUND 1: Exercise A sets, Exercise B sets. ROUND 2: same. Rest timer fires after completing one full round (after the last exercise in a round is checked off).
- '+ Add round' at the bottom adds a set to every exercise in the superset simultaneously.

Finishing a session:
- 'Finish workout' shows a summary: total duration, total working-set volume (weight × reps, warmup sets excluded), exercises completed.
- If prompt_save_as_template is true AND the session was NOT started from a template, a modal appears: 'Save this as a template?' with [Skip] and [Save Template] buttons, plus a 'Don't ask me again' checkbox. If the checkbox is checked on Skip, prompt_save_as_template is set to false. The option remains accessible as a secondary 'Save as template' button on the session summary screen regardless of this setting.
- Settings → Workouts contains a 'Prompt to save new workouts as templates' toggle that re-enables the prompt if turned off.
steps: Start a session from a template — verify all exercises are pre-loaded and ghost values appear from the previous session
Tap an exercise row to open the detail page — verify the set table renders with ghost-value fields
Tap a ghost value field — verify it activates and the ghost style clears for that field only
Mark a set complete — verify the rest timer starts and the next set row highlights
Navigate back to the session overview and return to the detail page — verify the rest timer is still running
Add a warmup set — verify it renders with a 'W' badge and does not count toward the completion indicator on the overview
Mark all sets of an exercise complete — verify the overview row shows '✓ 3/3 sets' (or similar)
Open a superset detail page — verify exercises are displayed interleaved by round, and the rest timer fires after each complete round
Finish a session started from a blank (no template) — verify the 'Save as template?' modal appears
Check 'Don't ask me again' and tap Skip — verify the modal no longer appears on the next blank session
Verify Settings → Workouts has a toggle to re-enable the prompt
Verify the 'Save as template' secondary button is still accessible on the session summary even when the prompt is disabled

features.title: Workout progress tracking — per-exercise trends, estimated 1RM, volume charts
features.description:
A Progress tab on the workouts page showing how each exercise has changed over time. Requires workouts-001 (first-class sets) to be complete. Warmup sets (is_warmup=true) are excluded from all calculations.

Endpoint: GET /workouts/progress/:exercise_id?limit=20 returns a time-series of sessions: [{session_date, sets: [{reps, weight, is_warmup, target_reps}]}] for the given exercise, ordered oldest to newest, excluding warmup sets.

Frontend — Progress tab:
- Lists all exercises the user has logged in at least 2 sessions, sorted by most recently logged.
- Each row shows the exercise name and a sparkline (tiny inline trend of max weight over last 5 sessions).
- Tapping an exercise opens a full-screen progress detail view with:
  (1) Max weight chart: line chart of the heaviest working set per session over time.
  (2) Estimated 1RM chart: Epley formula applied per session — 1RM ≈ weight × (1 + reps / 30). Calculated client-side, never stored. Only shown for exercises with reps ≤ 10 (high-rep sets produce unreliable 1RM estimates).
  (3) Volume chart: total working volume per session (sum of weight × reps across all working sets).
  (4) A 'Failed sets' indicator: sessions where logged reps < target_reps on any working set are marked with a small ✗ on the timeline.
- Toggle between the three chart types via a segmented control at the top of the detail view.
- Bodyweight exercises (weight=null): only the reps chart is shown (max reps per session, volume as total reps).
steps: Log 'Bench Press' across 3 sessions with increasing weight — navigate to Workouts → Progress
Verify 'Bench Press' appears in the list with a sparkline showing the upward trend
Tap 'Bench Press' — verify the max weight chart renders with one data point per session
Switch to the 1RM chart — verify estimated 1RM values are plausible (Epley formula) and match manual calculation for one session
Switch to the volume chart — verify total volume (weight × reps summed) is correct for each session
Log a session where reps < target_reps on one set — verify the failed set indicator (✗) appears on that session on the timeline
Log a bodyweight exercise — verify only the reps chart is available (no weight/1RM charts)
Log warmup sets alongside working sets — verify warmup sets are excluded from all chart calculations
Verify exercises with only 1 logged session do NOT appear in the progress list

features.title: Workout calendar integration — sessions on calendar, habits in time slots
features.description:
Logged workout sessions and scheduled workout habits both appear on the calendar. Requires habit-002 (scheduled_time field on habits) to be complete first.

Two things on the calendar:
(1) Workout habits with a scheduled_time render as chips in the correct time slot on their scheduled days (e.g. a Monday/Wednesday/Friday habit at 06:00 shows as a chip in the 6am row on those days). Habits without a scheduled_time remain as all-day chips — existing behaviour preserved.
(2) Completed workout sessions appear on the day they were performed (using started_at date). Chip shows the template name or session name. Tapping opens the session log (read-only view of exercises and sets logged).

The calendar does not distinguish between a 'planned workout day' and a 'rest day' — it shows what was actually logged and what is scheduled via habits. Users interpret the gap themselves.

Create a workout habit without a scheduled_time — verify it renders as an all-day chip (unchanged behaviour)

Tap the session chip — verify it opens a read-only session log showing all logged exercises and sets
Verify a day with both a scheduled habit chip and a completed session chip renders both without overlap

features.title: Weekly meal planner — drag recipes onto calendar days, auto-generate grocery list
features.description: A meal planning view where users assign recipes to specific days of the week (breakfast, lunch, dinner slots). From the plan, a single 'Generate grocery list' button aggregates all ingredients across the week's recipes, deduplicating overlapping items, and creates or appends to a grocery list. New domain: meal_plans table (id, household_id, week_start DATE) and meal_plan_entries (id, plan_id, recipe_id, date, meal_slot ['breakfast'|'lunch'|'dinner'|'snack']). Frontend: a new /meal-planner route with a 7-column weekly grid. Recipes can be dragged from a sidebar recipe list onto day/slot cells. Slots already filled show the recipe name and a remove button. 'Plan this week' button pre-selects the current week; prev/next week navigation. 'Generate grocery list' button reuses the existing add_recipe_ingredients_to_list() service (grocery-001), called once per recipe in the plan.

features.title: Chore rotation system — recurring chores assigned to members on a rotating schedule
features.description: A chore rotation system built on top of the existing todo + assignment model. A 'chore' is a recurring todo with rotation_enabled=true and a rotation_members list (ordered list of user_ids). When the chore's recurrence fires (next occurrence is created), the assigned_to_user_id rotates to the next member in the list. Household members can see their assigned chores on the dashboard and on /todos filtered by 'Assigned to me'. New UI: a Chores section in Settings → Household (or a dedicated /chores page) where admins can create chore definitions — name, frequency, rotation_members ordered list, optional description. When a chore is marked complete, the next occurrence is generated (same as recurring todos) but assigned to the next person in rotation.

features.title: Shared household capture inbox — quick-add action items from conversations
features.description: A lightweight shared inbox where any household member can drop a 'we should deal with this' item. Not a messaging feature — it is a capture surface that converts to todos or household notes. New domain: household_inbox_items (id, household_id, created_by_user_id, text, created_at, resolved_at, converted_to_todo_id). Frontend: a bell/inbox icon in the header (separate from notifications) that opens a side panel showing all unresolved items. Any member can add a new item (text input + submit). Items can be: converted to a todo (opens the todo creation sheet pre-filled with the item text), archived/resolved (disappears from the inbox), or dismissed. The inbox is always household-shared scope — there is no personal inbox. Useful for: 'Call the plumber about the sink', 'We're almost out of coffee', 'Need to book summer travel'.

features.title: Savings goal progress tied to Teller account balance
features.description: Allow a goal to be linked to a specific Teller-synced budget account so the goal's progress bar reflects the actual account balance rather than a manually-updated figure. Extension of the financial_link JSONB on goals: new type 'account_balance' with fields account_id and target_balance. Progress = current_balance / target_balance (from BudgetAccount.current_balance, which is updated by Teller sync). When target_balance is reached, the goal auto-completes. Useful for: 'Save $10,000 in my emergency fund' linked to a specific savings account. Requires budget-018 (Teller linking) and budget-017 (account balance field) to be complete.

features.title: Garmin Connect integration — activity import and AI coach signal
features.description: Connect a Garmin account via the Garmin Health API (OAuth 2.0) to import daily activity summaries (steps, active minutes, calories, heart rate, sleep data). A background daily sync fetches the previous day's summary and upserts it. The AI coach reads recent activity data as an additional context signal: low activity + negative journal sentiment is surfaced as a pattern; strong workout streaks are noted during positive periods. New 'Garmin' section in Settings → Integrations. Activity data is personal scope only — never shared with household members.

features.title: Apple Health / Apple Watch sync — iOS-only activity import
features.description: Import activity data from Apple Health (HealthKit) into Hearth. HealthKit data is only accessible on-device via an iOS/iPadOS native app or a web app with HealthKit entitlements (requires Safari on iOS 16.4+). Two viable approaches: (1) A native iOS companion app (React Native or Swift) that reads HealthKit and POSTs summaries to the Hearth API — the most capable path but requires App Store submission; (2) A Safari web extension or PWA with HealthKit permissions — narrower data access but no App Store dependency. Data model is identical to health-001 (health_activity_summaries with source='apple_health'). This feature is gated on having a mobile presence — it cannot be done from the desktop web app. Garmin (health-001) is the recommended first health integration since it works via web OAuth with no native app requirement.

The AI has access to the user's existing goals and projects to avoid duplication. After the conversation, the user reviews the created goal/project before saving.

features.title: Home Assistant integration — voice commands via REST API
features.description: Expose a Home Assistant-compatible REST API that allows HA automations and the HA Assist voice assistant to interact with Hearth. Actions available via HA: add item to active grocery list, create a todo, check in a habit for today, query today's todo count. Each action maps to an existing Hearth API endpoint; HA calls them via a long-lived API token (new token type: integration_token with a restricted scope). No HA-side custom component needed — plain RESTful calls. Example: 'Hey Google, ask Home Assistant to add milk to the shopping list' → HA automation → POST /grocery-lists/{active_list_id}/items.
steps: Navigate to Settings → Integrations → Home Assistant
Generate an integration token — verify it appears once and cannot be retrieved again
Add the Hearth RESTful integration to HA configuration.yaml with the token and base URL
Restart HA — verify HA can GET /grocery-lists and receive a valid response
Create an HA automation that POSTs a grocery item — verify it appears in Hearth
Verify the integration token cannot access endpoints outside its declared scope
Revoke the token — verify subsequent HA calls return 401

features.title: Amazon Alexa skill — household voice control (self-build guide included)
features.description: A custom Alexa skill that exposes core Hearth actions to Alexa voice commands. Supported intents: AddGroceryItem, CreateTodo, CheckInHabit, QueryTodos ('Alexa, ask Hearth to add milk to the shopping list', 'Alexa, ask Hearth how many todos I have today'). Architecture: the Alexa skill calls a Lambda function (or a public Hearth endpoint directly via HTTPS) which authenticates via an integration token (same token system as voice-001). Account linking uses Alexa's account linking flow to associate the user's Hearth account with their Alexa account. The skill must be submitted to the Alexa Skill Store for certification before it can be used on consumer Echo devices. IMPORTANT: the skill is self-published by the developer — it does not need to be listed publicly; it can be submitted as a 'private' skill available only to the developer's Amazon account for testing. Self-build guide in docs/alexa-skill-setup.md covers: creating the skill in the Alexa Developer Console, deploying the Lambda, and testing on a physical Echo without HA hardware.
steps: Follow docs/alexa-skill-setup.md to create the skill in the Alexa Developer Console
Deploy the Lambda function with the Hearth integration token
Complete account linking — verify the Alexa app shows 'Hearth' as a linked skill
Say 'Alexa, ask Hearth to add milk to my shopping list' — verify the item appears in Hearth
Say 'Alexa, ask Hearth to create a todo: call the dentist' — verify the todo is created
Say 'Alexa, ask Hearth how many todos do I have today' — verify Alexa reads back the correct count
Test on a physical Echo device (or the Alexa app simulator) — verify responses are natural and brief
Revoke the integration token — verify Alexa commands return a graceful 'unable to connect' response

features.title: GoCardless bank linking — EU/UK alternative to Teller
features.description:
Integrate GoCardless (via their Bank Account Data API, formerly Nordigen) as the Open Banking connectivity layer for EU and UK users. Teller only covers US/CA institutions; GoCardless covers 2,300+ banks across 31 European countries using PSD2/Open Banking standards. The two integrations are parallel and non-exclusive — the user's region determines which is offered, or both can be shown with the appropriate one highlighted.

Backend: add gocardless_requisition_id and gocardless_account_id to budget_accounts (nullable, separate from teller_* columns). New service module at domains/budget/gocardless_service.py wrapping the GoCardless Bank Account Data API (REST, no SDK required — use httpx). A /budget/accounts/gocardless/link endpoint initiates the GoCardless authorisation flow: (1) create a requisition via POST /api/v2/requisitions/ specifying institution_id and redirect_uri; (2) redirect the user's browser to requisition.link for the bank's consent screen; (3) on redirect back, store the confirmed account_id against the budget_account. A background sync job calls GET /api/v2/accounts/{id}/transactions/ and upserts into budget_transactions deduplicated by gocardless_transaction_id (or the transaction's internalTransactionId). Account balances from GET /api/v2/accounts/{id}/balances/ overwrite current_balance (budget-017) when a sync runs.

GoCardless tokens: a GoCardless secret_id + secret_key pair (stored as encrypted env vars, not per-user) generates short-lived access tokens via POST /api/v2/token/new/. Tokens are cached in-process and refreshed automatically before expiry.

Region detection: show the 'Link account' flow with a country picker. If the selected country is US or CA, proceed with Teller; otherwise proceed with GoCardless. Both can coexist — a household might have a US account (Teller) and a French account (GoCardless) simultaneously.

Free tier limits: GoCardless Bank Account Data has a free tier (90-day transaction history, limited requisitions per month — check current pricing before launch). Exceeding the free tier is a cost passed through to the cloud hosting tier; self-hosted users supply their own GoCardless credentials.
steps: Navigate to Budget → Accounts → Link account and select a European country (e.g. France)
Verify the GoCardless institution picker appears with banks for that country
Select a bank — verify the browser is redirected to GoCardless's authorisation URL
Complete the bank consent flow — verify the redirect back creates a budget_account with gocardless_account_id set
Verify transactions from the past 90 days are imported and appear in the transaction list
Verify the account balance chip (budget-017) is populated from the GoCardless balances endpoint
Trigger a manual sync — verify new transactions appear and duplicates are not created (idempotent on internalTransactionId)
Verify a US institution selection still routes to the Teller flow (not GoCardless)
Disconnect the linked account — verify the gocardless_account_id and any cached tokens are cleared
Verify API token refresh: allow the cached GoCardless access token to expire (or force expiry) and confirm the next sync automatically fetches a fresh token without error

features.title: Docker Compose + NAS deployment — self-hosted packaging
features.description: Package Hearth as a Docker Compose stack for users who want to self-host on a NAS (Synology, QNAP, Unraid) or a home server. The infra/ directory already has a skeleton. Compose services: web (Next.js, built image), api (FastAPI, built image), db (Postgres 16), caddy (TLS reverse proxy with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt or local cert). Environment is configured via a single .env file. A first-run setup script generates SECRET_KEY and FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY automatically. Caddy config handles: the web → Next.js proxy, /api/* → FastAPI proxy, and TLS termination. Tailscale integration guide (optional): users can expose Hearth to their Tailscale network without opening ports to the public internet. Documentation in infra/NAS-DEPLOYMENT.md covering Synology Container Manager UI steps for non-technical users.
steps: Run `docker compose up` in infra/ — verify all four services start without errors
Navigate to https://localhost (or the configured domain) — verify the Hearth login page loads with a valid TLS certificate
Complete signup and verify the API calls succeed through the Caddy proxy
Run `docker compose exec api alembic upgrade head` — verify migrations apply cleanly
Stop and restart the stack — verify data persists (Postgres volume is mounted correctly)
Follow the Synology Container Manager steps in NAS-DEPLOYMENT.md on a real Synology — verify the stack runs
Verify the first-run setup script generates valid SECRET_KEY and FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY values
